While ensuring network security, this will enhance the on-chain data publishing and complex computation capabilities. * Full Implementation of Account Abstraction: Promote the implementation of EIP-8141, the account abstraction proposal, to achieve native support for smart contract wallets on Ethereum. This will be compatible with core functions such as transaction gas fee payment, quantum-resistant signature algorithms, and privacy protocols, significantly expanding the application boundaries of Ethereum. * Early Deployment of Quantum-Resistant Security: Optimize hash- and lattice-based quantum-resistant signature algorithms to address the potential risks of quantum computing. Upgrade EVM vectorization to solve the pain points of low efficiency and high on-chain resource consumption in existing quantum-resistant signatures, completing the underlying technical preparation for quantum security after Ethereum's implementation. * Overcoming Privacy and Storage Capacity Challenges: Continuously strengthen on-chain privacy support and focus on overcoming the technical difficulties of storage expansion, addressing Ethereum's shortcomings in storage capacity. Disclaimer: Jinse Finance, as a blockchain information platform, publishes articles for informational purposes only and should not be considered actual investment advice. Please establish sound investment principles and be sure to enhance your risk awareness.

data.title: Why do you always lose money on Polymarket?
data.description:
Do you know why you can't beat the leaders on Polymarket? Because they scrutinize the rules, nitpicking like lawyers reviewing contracts. In April 2026, a controversy on Polymarket regarding the Venezuelan leader caused a stir in the community. One market on Polymarket asked, "Who will be the leader of Venezuela at the end of 2026?" Many traders instinctively thought: Maduro is in a US prison, Delcy is running the cabinet in Caracas, and Delcy is clearly the one in power, so they bet on Delcy. However, the rules and supplementary explanations clearly state: "officially holds" refers to someone officially appointed and sworn in. The UN-recognized Venezuelan government has not formally removed or replaced Maduro, and official government information still identifies him as president. The rules also specifically add a clause: "A temporary authorization to exercise presidential powers does not equate to a transfer of the presidency." According to these rules, even if Maduro remains in a US prison, he is still the legitimate president of Venezuela. There are many similar examples: After Polymarket issued its stablecoin, the market debated "What is the FDV of the Polymarket token?": Is a stablecoin a token? A difference of just one word. Iranian uranium: The standard of "agreement," conditional statements vs. formal signing of an agreement. These cases share the same logic: at Polymarket, the rules are the core. However, when disputes arise regarding the rules, Polymarket has a complete adjudication process to resolve them. This article will introduce how this mechanism works and where it is similar to, and where there are fundamental differences between, and traditional courts. I. Polymarket's Adjudication Mechanism Ambiguity in the rule text not only causes pricing disagreements, but it can also escalate into formal disputes during settlement. Polymarket handles a large number of market settlements daily, with markets involving political statements, diplomatic pronouncements, and military actions being particularly prone to disputes. Disputes are actually a normal part of the market. Ambiguity creates pricing disagreements during the trading phase and escalates into disputes during the settlement phase; it's the same issue manifesting differently at two points in time. To resolve these disputes, Polymarket has established a comprehensive adjudication process, with two paths: normal settlement and dispute adjudication. Step 1: Submit a Propose When the market meets the settlement conditions, anyone can submit a resolution, stating whether the market should rule YES or NO. Submitting a proposal requires a 750 USDC deposit as collateral. This deposit serves as the submitter's endorsement of their judgment. If the market has no objections, the user who submitted the proposal will receive a 5 USDC reward. Currently, only 1782 users have submitted proposals in the market, and the most profitable user has accumulated $281,000.

Step Two: 2-Hour Challenge Window (Dispute)

After submitting the proposal, a 2-hour challenge period begins. This is the first fork in the entire process.

If no one raises an objection within 2 hours, the system assumes the proposal is correct, the market settles directly, and the process ends. Most markets follow this path.

If someone believes the proposal result is incorrect, they can submit a challenge within these 2 hours, which also requires staking 750 USDC. A successful challenge earns a bonus of 250 USDC.

Few users specialize in Dispute mode. The user who earned the most in the Dispute stage was 0xB7A, with a profit of $17,123. Step 3: A Discussion Period of Up to 48 Hours After entering the disputed phase, both sides proceed to the UMA Discord discussion stage. The purpose of this stage is to allow each party to submit arguments and evidence: interpretations of the rule text, relevant news reports, historical precedents, official statements—any material that can support their position can be presented at this stage. The discussion period lasts up to 48 hours and is the only stage in the entire process where reasons can be fully presented. The quality of this stage largely determines the direction of the subsequent vote. Step 4: 48-Hour Voting After the discussion, the voting phase for UMA token holders begins, divided into two 24-hour phases. The first phase is blind voting. Each voter must make an independent judgment based on their own understanding of the rules, rather than following the lead of large holders. The second phase is public voting. Votes not made public during this phase are considered abstentions and are invalidated. After the voting concludes, UMA sets two settlement thresholds that must be met simultaneously to complete the decision: Regarding participation: at least 5 million tokens are required to participate in the vote, ensuring sufficient representativeness of the decision. Regarding absolute consensus: the winning party must receive more than 65% of the votes, not simply a 51% majority. If both thresholds are not met simultaneously, the auction fails, and a re-vote is initiated, up to a maximum of four times. If no consensus is reached after four rounds, Polymarket has the right to intervene directly in the adjudication. Step 5: Automatic Settlement After the voting results are confirmed, the market automatically settles the accounts, and funds are distributed according to the results. There is no appeal, no retrial, and no opportunity for remedy. The entire dispute resolution process, from challenge submission to final settlement, typically takes less than a week.

II. Polymarket and Traditional Courts: Same Logic, Different Design

On the surface, the adjudication process in a polymarket is highly similar to that of a traditional court: both have a party making a claim, a party challenging the claim, a discussion and presentation phase, and finally, an adjudicator giving a result.

However, the two systems are fundamentally different in one design: the separation of powers.

1⃣The power of the court is isolated

In traditional courts, plaintiffs and defendants only have the right to present their cases, not the right to adjudicate. Judges only have the right to adjudicate, not the right to hold a vested interest. More importantly, judges and cases must remain independent. Once a judge has any conflict of interest with a case, they must recuse themselves and the case must be heard by another judge.

The adjudicator and the interested party are never the same person.

2⃣Polymarket lacks this isolation

UMA token holders are the adjudicators, but they can also hold positions in the controversial market. The direction of the adjudication directly affects their profits and losses.

The fact that the referee and the interested party are the same person is called a conflict of interest in traditional courts, which would be subject to mandatory recusal. However, in Polymarket, it's legal and normal. This design flaw is the root of the following two problems: 1. Why does the discussion phase fail? In court, the plaintiff's and defendant's positions are fixed from the moment the lawsuit is filed. Lawyers won't switch sides mid-trial, nor will they withdraw their statements simply because the opposing side is aggressive. Clear positions and defined roles form the basis of the entire debate. UMA Discord's discussions face two problems simultaneously. Herd mentality: Discussions are conducted openly and anonymously. Once an influential KOL expresses their opinion, it easily becomes a bandwagon effect. Many participants simply post a "P1" or "P2" without providing any reason.

Shift in Stance: Participants in the discussion simultaneously hold positions in the controversial market. When their positions change, their stance naturally shifts. This is why it's common to see opinions posted and then deleted on UMA Discord.

The root of both problems is the same: the arbitrator and the interested party are not separated. Courts use mandatory recusal to separate these two roles, ensuring the stability of stances during discussions. Polymarkets lack this separation.

2. Why are the rulings not transparent?

In court, the judge hears complete statements from both sides before making a ruling. The ruling specifies which side's arguments were adopted, the basis for the ruling, and the reasons for the ruling. The losing party may be dissatisfied, but at least they know where they went wrong and can strengthen their arguments next time.

These judgments form a system of precedents that can be studied, and subsequent judges, lawyers, and parties can cite them, thus making the standards of judgment verifiable, learnable, and predictable. After the UMA vote, there is only one result: YES or NO. The parties involved in the discussion do not know what the voters read, believed, or why they favored one side. If they win, they don't know which argument played a role; if they lose, they don't know where their persuasiveness was lacking. Because the logic of the judgments is never made public, the results of the disputes are difficult to learn and accumulate. The court's judgments form a system of precedents, and Polymarket's rulings leave only one result. III. In Conclusion Therefore, Polymarket is never simply a market for "guessing events correctly"; it is more like a system that translates real-world events into legal texts and then translates those legal texts into settlement results. Understanding the rules is just as important as conducting research. A leader's advantage often stems from a deep understanding of the rules—knowing what the system recognizes and what decisions it makes. Whoever recognizes the gap between "reality" and "rules" earlier has a greater chance of profiting from the price discrepancies created by misinterpretations, controversies, and emotions.
